annotations.label: Quantum field theory

anchor.snippet: is known as quantum field theory

annotations.note: Quantum field theory is a physics framework not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strings (string theory)

anchor.snippet: can also be modeled as one-dimensional objects called strings

annotations.note: The physics notion of fundamental strings is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bosonic string theory

anchor.snippet: The original version of string theory was bosonic string theory

annotations.note: Bosonic string theory is a physics model with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Supersymmetry

anchor.snippet: In theories with supersymmetry, each boson has a counterpart which is a fermion

annotations.note: Supersymmetry is a physics concept not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Versions of superstring theory

anchor.snippet: There are several versions of superstring theory: type I , type IIA , type IIB

annotations.note: The classification of superstring theories is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Critical spacetime dimensions

anchor.snippet: In bosonic string theory, spacetime is 26-dimensional

annotations.note: The critical-dimension result of string theory has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Compactification

anchor.snippet: Compactification is one way of modifying the number of dimensions in a physical theory

annotations.note: Mathlib has topological one-point/Stone–Čech compactification but not the distinct physics notion of curling up extra dimensions.

annotations.label: Calabi–Yau manifold

anchor.snippet: A Calabi–Yau manifold is a special space which is typically taken to be six-dimensional

annotations.note: A grep for Calabi/CalabiYau finds no manifold definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brane-world scenario

anchor.snippet: the so-called brane-world scenario

annotations.note: The brane-world scenario is physics with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: S-duality

anchor.snippet: One of the relationships that can exist between different string theories is called S-duality

annotations.note: S-duality is a physics relationship not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type I and SO(32) heterotic S-duality

anchor.snippet: Type I string theory turns out to be equivalent by S-duality

annotations.note: This specific string-duality statement is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: T-duality

anchor.snippet: T-duality states that a string propagating around a circle of radius R is equivalent

annotations.note: T-duality is a physics concept absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Duality (general)

anchor.snippet: the term duality refers to a situation where two seemingly different physical systems turn out to be equivalent

annotations.note: Physical duality (equivalence of physical systems) is not a Mathlib notion.

annotations.label: Montonen–Olive duality

anchor.snippet: Montonen–Olive duality is an example of an S-duality relationship between quantum field theories

annotations.note: Montonen–Olive duality is a QFT result not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brane

anchor.snippet: a brane is a physical object that generalizes the notion of a point particle to higher dimensions

annotations.note: Branes are physics objects with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: p-brane worldvolume

anchor.snippet: A p -brane sweeps out a ( p +1)-dimensional volume in spacetime called its worldvolume

annotations.note: The worldvolume of a p-brane is a physics notion not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: D-branes

anchor.snippet: D-branes are an important class of branes that arise when one considers open strings

annotations.note: D-branes are physics objects with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Branes as categorical objects

anchor.snippet: they are described as objects of certain categories

annotations.note: While category theory is in Mathlib, the description of branes as categorical objects (e.g. Fukaya/derived categories) is not.

annotations.label: M-theory unifies the five superstring theories

anchor.snippet: the five theories were just special limiting cases of an eleven-dimensional theory called M-theory

annotations.note: M-theory and its unification claim are physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nahm: maximal supersymmetric dimension is eleven

anchor.snippet: the maximum spacetime dimension in which one can formulate a consistent supersymmetric theory is eleven

annotations.note: Nahm's bound on supersymmetric dimensions is a physics theorem not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cremmer–Julia–Scherk eleven-dimensional supergravity

anchor.snippet: supergravity not only permits up to eleven dimensions but is in fact most elegant in this maximal number of dimensions

annotations.note: Eleven-dimensional supergravity is a physics construction absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eleven-dimensional supergravity has two-dimensional branes

anchor.snippet: eleven-dimensional supergravity includes two-dimensional branes

annotations.note: This supergravity brane-content statement is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wrapped membrane reproduces type IIA strings

anchor.snippet: this construction reproduces exactly the strings appearing in type IIA superstring theory

annotations.note: The membrane-to-IIA-string construction is physics with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Matrix

anchor.snippet: a matrix is a rectangular array of numbers or other data

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Matrix m n α` as functions `m → n → α`, exactly a rectangular array of entries.

annotations.label: Matrix model

anchor.snippet: a matrix model is a particular kind of physical theory whose mathematical formulation involves the notion of a matrix

annotations.note: A matrix model as a physical theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BFSS matrix model

anchor.snippet: One important example of a matrix model is the BFSS matrix model

annotations.note: The BFSS matrix model is physics with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Noncommutative geometry

anchor.snippet: a generalization of ordinary geometry in which mathematicians define new geometric notions using tools from noncommutative algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib has noncommutative rings/algebra but not the field of noncommutative geometry (e.g. spectral triples).

annotations.label: Connes–Douglas–Schwarz noncommutative field theory

anchor.snippet: some aspects of matrix models and M-theory are described by a noncommutative quantum field theory

annotations.note: The Connes–Douglas–Schwarz result is physics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Black hole

anchor.snippet: a black hole is defined as a region of spacetime in which the gravitational field is so strong that no particle or radiation can escape

annotations.note: Black holes are a general-relativity notion not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Entropy

anchor.snippet: entropy is a measure of the randomness or disorder of a physical system

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Shannon-entropy building block `negMulLog` and topological entropy, but no thermodynamic/statistical entropy of a physical system.

annotations.label: Boltzmann entropy

anchor.snippet: a precise definition of entropy as the natural logarithm of the number of different states of the molecules

annotations.note: Although `Real.log` exists, Boltzmann's S = k log W entropy is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Bekenstein area-entropy proposal

anchor.snippet: the entropy of a black hole is instead proportional to the surface area of its event horizon

annotations.note: The black-hole area-entropy proportionality is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bekenstein–Hawking formula

anchor.snippet: The Bekenstein–Hawking formula expresses the entropy S as

annotations.note: The Bekenstein–Hawking entropy formula is a physics result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strominger–Vafa derivation of black hole entropy

anchor.snippet: showed how to derive the Bekenstein–Hawking formula for certain black holes in string theory

annotations.note: The Strominger–Vafa entropy derivation is physics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extremal black hole

anchor.snippet: These are defined as black holes with the lowest possible mass compatible with a given charge

annotations.note: Extremal black holes are a physics notion not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalization of entropy result

anchor.snippet: the original result could be generalized to an arbitrary consistent theory of quantum gravity

annotations.note: This generalization of the entropy derivation is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AdS/CFT correspondence

anchor.snippet: string theory is in some cases equivalent to a quantum field theory

annotations.note: The AdS/CFT correspondence is a physics conjecture/result not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Anti-de Sitter space

anchor.snippet: anti-de Sitter space is a mathematical model of spacetime in which the notion of distance between points

annotations.note: Anti-de Sitter space (a Lorentzian constant-curvature spacetime) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three-dimensional anti-de Sitter space

anchor.snippet: The resulting geometric object is three-dimensional anti-de Sitter space

annotations.note: Three-dimensional anti-de Sitter space is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Boundary theory equivalence

anchor.snippet: the boundary of anti-de Sitter space can be regarded as the

annotations.note: The AdS-boundary/CFT equivalence is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hawking radiation

anchor.snippet: black holes are not completely black but emit a dim radiation due to quantum effects near the event horizon

annotations.note: Hawking radiation is a physics result with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Black hole information paradox

anchor.snippet: came to be known as the black hole information paradox

annotations.note: The information paradox is a physics problem not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AdS/CFT resolves the information paradox

anchor.snippet: The AdS/CFT correspondence resolves the black hole information paradox

annotations.note: This physics resolution claim has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Shear viscosity to entropy density ratio

anchor.snippet: the shear viscosity and volume density of entropy, should be approximately equal to a certain universal constant

annotations.note: The η/s viscosity-to-entropy bound is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Superfluid

anchor.snippet: A superfluid is a system of electrically neutral atoms that flows without any friction

annotations.note: Superfluidity is a physics phenomenon not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mirror manifolds and mirror symmetry

anchor.snippet: the manifolds are called mirror manifolds, and the relationship between the two physical theories is called mirror symmetry

annotations.note: Mirror symmetry and mirror manifolds are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Enumerative geometry and algebraic varieties

anchor.snippet: Enumerative geometry studies a class of geometric objects called algebraic varieties which are defined by the vanishing of polynomials

annotations.note: Mathlib has schemes and vanishing/zero loci of polynomials (`zeroLocus`) but no general classical 'algebraic variety' nor enumerative geometry.

annotations.label: Clebsch cubic

anchor.snippet: the Clebsch cubic illustrated on the right is an algebraic variety defined using a certain polynomial of degree three in four variables

annotations.note: The Clebsch cubic surface is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cayley–Salmon: 27 lines on a cubic surface

anchor.snippet: there are exactly 27 straight lines that lie entirely on such a surface

annotations.note: The 27-lines-on-a-cubic-surface theorem is not in Mathlib (Cayley matches refer to Cayley–Hamilton/Cayley graphs).

annotations.label: Schubert: 2,875 lines on the quintic

anchor.snippet: there are exactly 2,875 such lines

annotations.note: The count of lines on a quintic threefold is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Katz: degree-two curves on the quintic

anchor.snippet: lie entirely in the quintic is 609,250

annotations.note: The 609,250 degree-two-curve count is an enumerative-geometry result not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Candelas et al.: degree-three rational curves

anchor.snippet: a six-dimensional Calabi–Yau manifold can contain exactly 317,206,375 curves of degree three

annotations.note: The Gromov–Witten degree-three curve count is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Homological mirror symmetry program

anchor.snippet: the homological mirror symmetry program of Maxim Kontsevich

annotations.note: Kontsevich's homological mirror symmetry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: SYZ conjecture

anchor.snippet: the SYZ conjecture of Andrew Strominger, Shing-Tung Yau, and Eric Zaslow

annotations.note: The SYZ conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Group

anchor.snippet: the collection of these symmetries satisfies certain technical properties making it into what mathematicians call a group

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Group` class is the standard axiomatization of a group.

annotations.label: Dihedral group of order 6

anchor.snippet: the group is known as the dihedral group of order 6 because it has six elements

annotations.note: `DihedralGroup 3` is exactly the dihedral group of order 6, a special case of the parametrized family.

annotations.label: Finite group

anchor.snippet: if there are only finitely many symmetries, it is called a finite group

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses finite groups via `[Group G] [Finite G]` rather than a single dedicated 'finite group' definition.

annotations.label: Finite simple groups

anchor.snippet: These are finite groups that may be used as building blocks for constructing arbitrary finite groups

annotations.note: `IsSimpleGroup` defines simple groups in general; finite simple groups are obtained by adding `[Finite G]`.

annotations.label: Classification of finite simple groups

anchor.snippet: the classification of finite simple groups , a mathematical theorem that provides a list of all possible finite simple groups

annotations.note: The classification of finite simple groups is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Monster group

anchor.snippet: The largest sporadic group, the so-called monster group , has over 10 53 elements

annotations.note: A grep for Monster finds no Monster sporadic group in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modular functions and the j-function

anchor.snippet: This object belongs to a special class of functions called modular functions

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes (slash-invariant) modular forms, but not the specific modular j-function used in moonshine.

annotations.label: McKay–Thompson observation (moonshine)

anchor.snippet: are related to numbers that appear in a formula for the j -function

annotations.note: The McKay–Thompson moonshine observation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Borcherds' proof of monstrous moonshine

anchor.snippet: Richard Borcherds constructed a bridge between the theory of modular functions and finite groups

annotations.note: Borcherds' monstrous moonshine theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mathieu group M24 moonshine

anchor.snippet: discovered connections between a different sporadic group, the Mathieu group M 24

annotations.note: A grep for Mathieu finds no Mathieu group in Mathlib, so Mathieu moonshine is absent.

annotations.label: Umbral moonshine

anchor.snippet: proposed a generalization of this moonshine phenomenon called umbral moonshine

annotations.note: Umbral moonshine is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nordström: five-dimensional unification

anchor.snippet: gravity in five dimensions describes both gravity and electromagnetism in four

annotations.note: Nordström/Kaluza–Klein five-dimensional unification is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lovelace: consistency requires dimension 26

anchor.snippet: there is an inconsistency unless the dimension of the theory is 26

annotations.note: Lovelace's dimension-26 consistency result is physics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No wrong-sign states below dimension 26

anchor.snippet: there are no wrong-sign propagating states in dimensions less than or equal to 26

annotations.note: The no-ghost (wrong-sign state) theorem is a string-physics result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fermion theory critical dimension is 10

anchor.snippet: In the fermion theories, the critical dimension was 10

annotations.note: The critical dimension 10 of superstrings is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Yoneya: massless spin-two graviton

anchor.snippet: all the known string theories included a massless spin-two particle that obeyed the correct Ward identities to be a graviton

annotations.note: The massless spin-two graviton result is physics with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Friedan: equations of motion from renormalization group

anchor.snippet: the equations of motions of string theory, which are generalizations of the Einstein equations of general relativity

annotations.note: Friedan's RG-derived string equations of motion are physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Witten: quantum gravity and chiral fermions

anchor.snippet: most theories of quantum gravity could not accommodate chiral fermions like the neutrino

annotations.note: This chiral-fermion observation in quantum gravity is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Green–Schwarz: gauge group SO(32)

anchor.snippet: restricted the gauge group of the type I string theory to be SO(32)

annotations.note: The Green–Schwarz anomaly-cancellation result fixing SO(32) is physics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Calabi–Yau compactifications preserve supersymmetry

anchor.snippet: the Calabi–Yau manifolds are the compactifications that preserve a realistic amount of supersymmetry

annotations.note: This supersymmetry-preservation statement about Calabi–Yau compactifications is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Number of vacuum states

anchor.snippet: String theory as it is currently understood has an enormous number of vacuum states, typically estimated to be around 10 500

annotations.note: The string landscape vacuum count is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Anthropic principle

anchor.snippet: The anthropic principle is the idea that some of the numbers appearing in the laws of physics are not fixed by any fundamental principle

annotations.note: The anthropic principle is a physics/philosophy idea not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Swampland conjecture on de Sitter vacua

anchor.snippet: a controversial conjecture which would imply that no such universe exists

annotations.note: The de Sitter swampland conjecture is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Background independence

anchor.snippet: it is background independent , meaning that the formulation of the theory does not in any way privilege a particular spacetime geometry

annotations.note: Background independence is a physics concept not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heterotic string theory

anchor.snippet: two flavors of heterotic string theory ( SO (32) and E 8 × E 8 )

annotations.note: Heterotic string theories with their SO(32) and E8×E8 gauge groups are physics not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Winding number (string on a circle)

anchor.snippet: The number of times the string winds around a circle is called the winding number

annotations.note: The string-theoretic winding number along a compact extra dimension is a physics notion; Mathlib has a winding number for plane loops but not this.

annotations.label: T-duality momentum/winding exchange

anchor.snippet: If a string has momentum p and winding number n in one description, it will have momentum n and winding number p in the dual description

annotations.note: The exchange of momentum and winding number under T-duality is physics, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirichlet boundary condition

anchor.snippet: The letter "D" in D-brane refers to a certain mathematical condition on the system known as the Dirichlet boundary condition

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general formalization of Dirichlet boundary conditions for PDEs.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic space

anchor.snippet: It is closely related to hyperbolic space , which can be viewed as a disk

annotations.note: Mathlib has hyperbolic trigonometric functions but no formalization of hyperbolic space as a Riemannian manifold.

annotations.label: Minkowski space

anchor.snippet: it looks just like Minkowski space , the model of spacetime used in non-gravitational physics

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Minkowski space with its Lorentzian inner product as a named structure.

annotations.label: Quintic Calabi–Yau manifold

anchor.snippet: how many lines can be drawn on a quintic Calabi–Yau manifold , which is defined by a polynomial of degree five

annotations.note: The quintic Calabi–Yau threefold is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetry of a geometric shape

anchor.snippet: Each of these operations is called a symmetry

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes symmetries as permutations/isometries (e.g. `Equiv.Perm`, `IsometryEquiv`), but not the bare geometric notion of a symmetry of a shape.

annotations.label: Sporadic groups

anchor.snippet: The latter groups are called the "sporadic" groups

annotations.note: The 26 sporadic simple groups are not formalized as a named class in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Irreducible representation

anchor.snippet: namely, the dimensions of its irreducible representations

annotations.note: Irreducible representations correspond to simple modules over the group algebra; Mathlib has `Representation` and `IsSimpleModule`.

annotations.label: Fourier series

anchor.snippet: namely, the coefficients of its Fourier series

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Fourier series and their coefficients on the circle via `fourierCoeff`.

annotations.label: Gamma function

anchor.snippet: a special mathematical function whose poles are evenly spaced on half the real line—the gamma function

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the gamma function `Real.Gamma` (and `Complex.Gamma`), including its pole structure.

annotations.label: Orbifold

anchor.snippet: the physical properties of orbifolds , distinctive geometrical singularities allowed in string theory

annotations.note: Orbifolds are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: physical systems evolve in time according to the Schrödinger equation

annotations.note: The Schrödinger equation is a physics PDE not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural logarithm

anchor.snippet: a precise definition of entropy as the natural logarithm of the number of different states

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the natural logarithm `Real.log`.
